Read the spec — a tracking-first workout tracker with AI as a planning layer, structured inputs over Markdown, deviation as first-class, and no gamification. Asked what the user wants to do next.
how do you imagine the app? high level

Described a high-level vision: one main "Today" screen you live in during workouts, a lightweight plan/calendar that repairs forward, and a coach/config surface. AI is invisible by default, surfacing as ghost targets, inline nudges, and a single "what should I do?" action. Offered to build a clickable HTML mockup.
make a wireframe runnable prototype use the multiple skills I added for you: ios-hig-design, mobile-ios-design, liquid-glass-design run it on my computere asap use sonnet agents to do the coding
Noted that only `liquid-glass-design` is installed; the other two skills aren't found under those names, so iOS HIG principles will be applied directly. Began building a web prototype.

Built and opened a single-file web prototype with three tabs (Today, Plan, Coach), CSS-based Liquid Glass styling, and working interactions including coach nudges, plan repair, and Markdown export. Noted it's a web emulation and offered to build a native SwiftUI version next.

no, build it as an actual ios app -- I have xcode and everything! don't use card containers -- those are for the web and look bad on iphone -- bleed-edge instead. I do circuits or supersets very often so make that representable on the UI. the UI should be tik-tok like where I only see what set I need to do next and perhaps cues or something that can be attached by the coach for the exercise. I should be able to tap next or provide very easily feedback on how easy/hard it felt or write a note or whatever. open it on my ios simulator when ready

Confirmed Xcode 26.6 / iOS 26.5 SDK with real Liquid Glass APIs. Set up the project via xcodegen and began having the Sonnet agent write SwiftUI for a full-bleed, TikTok-style set runner with circuit/superset support, coach cues, quick feedback pills, and native polish. Will build, fix errors, and launch on the iOS simulator automatically.
